coredata.dc:title: Efficient visual adsorption of Pb2+ by nanocellulose/sodium alginate microspheres with fluorescence sensitivity
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hanyu
Chen, Yehong
Dorsel, Padonou-Kengue Patrick
Wu, Chaojun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method using tempo-oxidized cellulose nanocrystals (TOCNC) and polyethylenimine (PEI). The CDs were self-assembled with a microsphere adsorbent prepared using TOCNC and sodium alginate (SA). CDs-TOCNC/SA—an environmentally friendly, fluorescent-sensitive, and recyclable microsphere adsorbent—was obtained. FTIR analysis showed that PEI successfully modified the CDs. In addition, the fluorescence quenching of CDs was observed when the concentration of Pb2+ was 0.0001–100 mg/L, indicating that CDs can dynamically monitor Pb2+. CDs-TOCNC/SA can produce blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light and selectively and efficiently adsorb Pb2+. When the concentration of Pb2+ was 0.0001–100 mg/L, fluorescence quenching of the adsorbent was observed, indicating that CDs-TOCNC/SA could visually adsorb Pb2+. The adsorption isotherm and kinetic parameters show that the adsorption process conforms to the Langmuir isotherm model at 298 K, and the maximum adsorption capacity of Pb2+ was 190.1 mg/g at pH = 5. Moreover, CDs-TOCNC/SA could still obtain 78.99 % Pb2+ after five sorption-desorption cycles. The adsorption mechanism may involve ion exchange, electrostatic attraction, intra-particle diffusion, and chemical complexation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocellulose
Carbon dots
Visual adsorption of Pb2+

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of adsorption properties of CDs-TOCNC/SA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The QUTJBZ Program (No. 2022JBZ01-05) financially supported funding this article.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of preparation of fluorescent gel spheres.

(a) TEM image of CDs. (b) UV–vis spectrum, CDs solution under ultraviolet light image, see illustration. (c) Emission spectra of CDs excited at different wavelengths. (d) Emission spectra of CDs in different metal ions. (e) FT-IR spectra of CDs. (f) The fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CDs varies with changing Pb2+ ion concentration. (g) The fluorescence quenching degree of the synthesized CDs varies with the Pb2+ ion concentration (from left to right, 0.0001 mg/L to 100 mg/L).
(a), (d) SEM images of TOCNC; (b), (e) CDs-TOCNC/SA; and (c), (f) EDS spectrum analysis of CDs-TOCNC/SA.

(a) The effect of pH value on the adsorption of Pb2+ (298 K, 12 h, 20 mg), (b) the effect of different adsorbent dosages on the adsorption efficiency of Pb2+ (pH = 5, 298 K, 12 h), (c) the effect of adsorption time on the adsorption efficiency of Pb2+ (pH = 5, 298 K, 200 min, 20 mg), and (d) the effect of temperature on the adsorption of Pb2+ (pH = 5, 298 K, 200 min, 20 mg). (e) Competitive adsorption of Pb2+ with partial metal ions (pH = 5, 298 K, 200 min, 60 mg). (f) Recyclability of CDs-TOCNC/SA (pH = 5, 298 K, 200 min, 60 mg).

(a) Pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting curve (pH = 5, 298 K, 200 min, 20 mg), (b) pseudo-second-order dynamic fitting curve (pH = 5, 298 K, 200 min, 20 mg), and (c) intraparticle diffusion model (pH = 5, 298 K, 200 min, 20 mg).
(a) Langmuir adsorption isotherm (pH = 5, 200 min, 20 mg). (b) Freundlich adsorption isotherm (pH = 5, 200 min, 20 mg).

FT-IR spectra: (a) XRD curves, and (b) TGA of (c) SA, TOCNC, CDs-TOCNC/SA, and CDs-TOCNC/SA + Pb2+. (d) N2 adsorption-desorption diagram of CDs-TOCNC/SA. (e) Fluorescence quenching before (left) and after (right) Pb2+ adsorption by CDs-TOCNC/SA. (f) The adsorption capacity and fluorescence quenching of CDs-TOCNC/SA for Pb2+ with different concentrations.
(a) XPS measurements of CDs-TOCNC/SA and CDs-TOCNC/SA + Pb2+. (b) Pb 4f spectrum, (c) Ca 2p spectrum, (d) C1s spectrum, (e) N1s spectrum, and (f) O1s spectrum.
Adsorption kinetics parameters of CDs-TOCNC/SA for Pb2+.
Equilibrium isotherm model parameters for Pb2+ by CDs-TOCNC/SA.
Comparison of Pb2+ adsorption properties between CDs-TOCNC/SA and previous adsorbents.

head.ce:title: Efficient visual adsorption of Pb2+ by nanocellulose/sodium alginate microspheres with fluorescence sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, Shandong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method using tempo-oxidized cellulose nanocrystals (TOCNC) and polyethylenimine (PEI). The CDs were self-assembled with a microsphere adsorbent prepared using TOCNC and sodium alginate (SA). CDs-TOCNC/SA—an environmentally friendly, fluorescent-sensitive, and recyclable microsphere adsorbent—was obtained. FTIR analysis showed that PEI successfully modified the CDs. In addition, the fluorescence quenching of CDs was observed when the concentration of Pb2+ was 0.0001–100 mg/L, indicating that CDs can dynamically monitor Pb2+. CDs-TOCNC/SA can produce blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light and selectively and efficiently adsorb Pb2+. When the concentration of Pb2+ was 0.0001–100 mg/L, fluorescence quenching of the adsorbent was observed, indicating that CDs-TOCNC/SA could visually adsorb Pb2+. The adsorption isotherm and kinetic parameters show that the adsorption process conforms to the Langmuir isotherm model at 298 K, and the maximum adsorption capacity of Pb2+ was 190.1 mg/g at pH = 5. Moreover, CDs-TOCNC/SA could still obtain 78.99 % Pb2+ after five sorption-desorption cycles. The adsorption mechanism may involve ion exchange, electrostatic attraction, intra-particle diffusion, and chemical complexation.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are key pollutants in industrial wastewater [1]. Heavy metal ions are almost impossible to biodegrade; they accumulate and migrate along the food chain, eventually endangering ecological safety and human health [2].

Lead is widely used as a general metal in battery manufacturing, electroplating, metal surface treatment, and welding, among other applications [3–5]. Therefore, lead ions are inevitably released into the water and cause pollution. Lead ions in the human body not only affect the intellectual development of people but also cause digestive and endocrine disorders and destroy immune system function [6–8]. Heavy metal removal technologies mainly involve precipitation, adsorption, and filtration, among other methods [9–11]. Of these, the adsorption method is considered the most practical and efficient method owing to its low cost and simple operation [12]. In recent years, with gradual improvements in green environmental protection requirements, the development of biodegradable nanocellulose-based heavy metal adsorbents has attracted the attention of many researchers for the study of toxic pollutant adsorbents [13].

Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) not only have the biodegradability and biocompatibility of natural cellulose but also the advantages of a high aspect ratio, high strength, and high specific surface area, so they can be used as nanoreinforced materials with mechanical properties [14,15]. Moreover, CNCs also have a property that other types of cellulose nanomaterials do not: their high crystallinity, which results in higher stiffness and strength as well as a smaller size, make them true nanomaterials [16]. In addition, CNC contains many active OH groups that can be modified, among which TOCNC uses TEMPO to oxidize the hydroxyl groups on its surface to carboxyl groups. Compared with CNC, the carboxyl group introduced into the cellulose main chain can further improve the adsorption performance of heavy metal removal [17].

As a new type of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) not only have fluorescence characteristics similar to those of other semiconductor quantum dots but also have the advantages of biocompatibility, water solubility, chemical stability, and low toxicity. Owing to these advantages, CDs have been widely used in the fields of medical diagnosis, biological imaging, catalysis, photovoltaic devices, and sensor technology [18]. In addition, the fluorescence of CDs is selectively quenched by heavy metal irons due to the electrostatic attraction and chemical complexation between heavy metal ions and the surface functional groups of CDs and graphite-like nuclei [19,20]. In conclusion, heavy metal ions can be effectively captured by CDs in water, and the detection of heavy metal ions can be realized by reducing the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. Based on this aspect, many studies have introduced CDs into gels or membranes to detect or visually adsorb heavy metal ions [21]. Lv et al. [22] synthesized CDs in bacterial cellulose membranes via biosynthesis and demonstrated that the resulting material has a sensitive fluorescence response to Fe3+. Chen et al. [23] synthesized a nitrogen-doped carbon point cross-linked nanocellulose hydrogel (NCDs-CNFgel) by a one-pot method and then obtained an NCDs-CNF/CSgel by solvent replacement with chitosan (CS). The adsorption capacities of Cu2+ and Cr6+ are 148.30 and 294.46 mg/g, respectively.

In this study, nitrogenous CDs were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method using TOCNC and PEI as raw materials. TOCNC/SA gel microspheres were prepared by Ca2+ crosslinking TOCNC with sodium alginate (SA). CDs-TOCNC/SA gel microspheres were prepared by self-assembling the electrostatic interactions between the CDs and TOCNC/SA hydrogel spheres. Finally, CDs-TOCNC/SA aerogel microspheres were prepared by freeze-drying. This method is green, easy to prepare, and reproducible. The adsorption mechanism of CDs-TOCNC/SA was proposed by studying the adsorption kinetics characteristics of CDs-TOCNC/SA and XPS peak fitting. The separation, recovery, and reuse of CDs-TOCNC/SA are also studied. The visual adsorption of Pb2+ was also demonstrated.

TOCNC were purchased from Tianjin Muelves Biotechnology Co. Ltd. The carboxyl content was 1.8 mmol/g. Polyethylene imine (PEI; 70,000 Mw, Macklin), sodium alginate (SA; AR grade, Aladdin), sodium hydroxide (NaOH; AR grade, Aladdin), Pb2+ standard solution (1000 μg/mL), and solute HNO3 (1 mol/L) were purchased from the National Nonferrous Metal Electronic Materials Analysis and Testing Center GB (Beijing) Inspection and Certification Co. Ltd.

PEI-modified CDs were prepared using a one-pot hydrothermal method. TOCNC was first mixed 1:1 with PEI. 175 mL deionized water was added to the mixture, which was then stirred for 2 h. Then the mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined high-pressure reactor (200 mL) and heated to 200 °C for 24 h.

A mixture of 1 g TOCNC and 1 g SA was magnetically stirred for 2 h, followed by a ultrasonic treatment for 30 min to make the mixture more even. The mixture was then dropped into a 5 % CaCl2 solution using a syringe and magnetically stirred to form a TOCNC/SA hydrogel. The obtained TOCNC/SA hydrogel microspheres were immersed in Ca2+ solution for 24 h to ensure complete crosslinking of microspheres. Then, the surface was washed three times with deionized water to remove excess Ca2+. The washed TOCNC/SAgel was soaked in deionized water using an atomic absorption spectrophotometer to test the presence of Ca2+ in the solution. If Ca2+ was present, the TOCNC/SAgel was washed further; otherwise, it was soaked in CDs for 24 h, and CDs-TOCNC/SA was prepared by electrostatic self-assembly. Finally, CDs-TOCNC/SA aerogel microspheres were obtained after washing and freeze-drying.

The fluorescence properties of the prepared CDs and CDs-TOCNC/SA are detected using a fluorescence spectrophotometer and steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (F-7100, Japan). For the synthesized CDs and CDs-TOCNC/SA, we used Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to characterize them in the band of 4000–450 cm−1. The surface area of CDs-TOCNC/SA was determined using the Brunauer-Emmett Teller (BET) method based on N2 adsorption. The lead ion concentration was determined using a GGX-600 (Beijing General Instruments, China) atomic absorption spectrometer (AAS).

The adsorption of Pb2+ ions on CD-TOCNC/SA gel spheres was determined by batch experiments. The CD-TOCNC/SA samples were each immersed in a 50 mL conical flask containing different concentrations of Pb2+ ionic solutions. The conical flask was fixed to a constant temperature shaker to set the temperature and residence time. The 10 mL solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter head filter, and its concentration was measured using a GGX660 atomic absorption spectrophotometer. All adsorption data were repeated three times and averaged.

Adsorption isothermal experiment: First, 50 mL Pb2+ solution with different initial concentrations (50, 75, 100, 125, and 150 mg/L) was placed in a 50 mL conical bottle. Next, CD-TOCNC/SA (approximately 20 mg) was added to each flask. Finally, all reaction systems were placed in a thermostatic oscillating water bath for 12 h and filtered using a solution filter membrane (0.22 μm) in each conical flask. The concentrations were measured, and the corresponding Qe was calculated.

The equation of equilibrium adsorption capacity Qe is as follows:
(1)
Q e
=
C 0
−
C e
× V
m

The adsorption quantity Qt of adsorption time t can be calculated as follows:
(2)
Q t
=
C t
−
C e
× V
c 0
where C0 (mg/L) is the initial solution concentration. The concentration at which adsorption reaches equilibrium is expressed by Ce (mg/L). The amount of CDs-TOCNC/SA is m (g). The volume of the Pb2+ solution is V (L). The concentration of Pb2+ in the solution at time t is Ct.

The adsorption efficiency of Pb2+ ion can be calculated by the following equation:
(3)
η =
C 0
−
C t
C 0
× 100 %

Based on the above experiments, mixed solutions of Pb2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, and Fe3+ with concentrations of 50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L, respectively, were prepared, and the pH was adjusted to 5. Then, 0.06 g CDs-TOCNC/SA was added to the mixed solution. At 25 °C, the supernatant was obtained by centrifugation and filtered through a filter membrane with a diameter of 0.45 μm. Finally, the adsorption capacity was calculated.

50 mL of lead ion solution with a concentration of 50 mg /L was configured, and its pH was adjusted to 5. Then, 60 mg of CDs-TOCNC/SA was added. After that, the reaction was brought into effect in a water bath at a constant temperature 25 °C, oscillating at 180 rpm for 2 h. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation. The remaining CDs-TOCNC/SA loaded with lead was immersed in 50 mL of 0.3 M HCl solution for 1 h. After that, the above CDs-TOCNC/SA is washed with deionized water and centrifuged, and then CDs-TOCNC/SA was obtained by centrifugation and dried in an oven at 80 °C. After cooling, the adsorption experiment of the first step was repeated. A 0.45 μm filter membrane was used to filter the supernatant obtained each time. Finally, the concentration of lead ions remaining in the solution was measured and the adsorption capacity was measured and calculated.

As shown in Fig. 1 , CDs were first synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method using TOCNC and PEI as raw materials. Second, the TOCNC and SA were cross-linked into pellets using calcium ions. Finally, the spheres were immersed in the CDs solution to compound CDs onto TOCNC/SA by electrostatic interaction, which was used as the fluorescence probe for Pb2+.

The surface morphology of the CDs is shown in the TEM image (Fig. 2 (a)). It can be seen that the CDs particles are spherical and evenly distributed. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the absorption bands centered at 277 nm and 340 nm can be observed in the UV absorption spectrum of CDs. The figure shows that the prepared CDs emitted blue fluorescence. Under different excitation wavelengths, the position of the emission peak of the CDs was slightly offset in a wide excitation wavelength range (Fig. 2(c)). In other words, CDs exhibit slight excitation-dependent emission. Meanwhile, when the excitation wavelength was 380 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs reached approximately 2500. The emission behavior of synthetic CDs is based on excitation because a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups have local energy levels on the surface [24]. The fluorescence properties of Pb2+ decreased the most when CDs were exposed to different heavy metal ions, while the fluorescence properties of Fe3+, Mn2+ and Cu2+ were less affected (Fig. 2(d)) [25,26]. T This may be because Pb2+ has the strongest ability to acquire electrons. Therefore, it is possible that Pb2+ attracts more transition electrons from the fluorescent molecules of CDs to Pb2+, thus generating the most obvious fluorescence quenching signal. The FTIR analysis of Fig. 2(e) shows that NH had a tensile vibration at 3340 cm−1 and NH had a bending vibration at 1641 cm−1. This proves that PEI successfully modified the CDs. In addition, the fluorescence intensity had a good linear relationship with the Pb2+ concentration (R2 = 0.9908), and the concentration of the Pb2+ solution could be detected semi-quantitatively (Fig. 2(f)). Therefore, CDs can be used as sensitive probes for Pb2+. As shown in Fig. 7(g), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreases with increasing Pb2+ concentration.

SEM. studied the morphology and structure of the synthesized fluorescent aerogel. As can be seen SEM studied the morphology and structure of the synthesized fluorescent aerogel. As can be seen from the figure, TOCNC presents a state of sheet aggregation with a smooth surface and no interleaved void structure (Fig. 3(a), (d)). However, the surface of CDs-TOCNC/SA is attached to small particle CDs of <1 μm, and many fibers are interwoven pore structures that can be observed after amplification, including micron and nanometer pore sizes (Fig. 3(b), (e)). In addition, according to the EDS energy spectrum analysis, the surface of CDs-TOCNC/SA contained N and Ca elements (Fig. 3(c), (d)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of adsorption properties of CDs-TOCNC/SA
ce:section.ce:para: The equilibrium adsorption capacities of CDs-TOCNC/SA at different pH values are shown in Fig. 4 (a). The adsorption capacity of Pb2+ increased with increasing pH under a constant adsorbent dosage. When the pH value is 1, CDs-TOCNC/SA has almost no adsorption capacity for Pb2+ because the competitive adsorption of the H+ and metal ions in the solution affects the binding of the adsorbent to Pb2+. Excessive H+ ions protonize the surface of the adsorbent and protonate the -NH2 to -NH3 + on the surface of the adsorbent, resulting in electrostatic repulsion with Pb2+. Therefore, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent is limited; this is not conducive to the adsorption process [27]. When the pH was 3, the adsorption capacity and efficiency sharply increased. Up to pH 7, the process increased only slightly, with a maximum removal efficiency of 100 %. It is worth noting that when pH < 6, the ions in the solution were mainly Pb2+ and PbNO3 +; this is conducive to the reaction with the CD-TOCNC/SA surface functional groups. However, when pH > 6, the Pb2+ in the solution precipitates as Pb (OH)2, which affects the detection of CD-TOCNC/SA adsorption performance. Therefore, the Pb2+ removal efficiency of CDs-TOCNC/SA was incorrectly assumed to be approximately 100 %. To exclude this effect, it may be assumed that pH 5 is the best condition for adsorption [28].

The influence of the amount of CDs-TOCNC/SA on the adsorption performance of Pb2+ is shown in Fig. 4(b). The Pb2+ removal rate of CDs-TOCNC/SA increased from 52 % to 99 % when the dosage increased from 0.2 g/L to 1.0 g/L. When the adsorbent dosage was 0.6 g/L, the Pb2+ removal reached 93 %. Although a 99 % removal rate was obtained at a higher load, the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent decreased. This is due to the presence of excessive adsorbents and a low concentration of Pb2+, leading to a large number of active adsorption sites being empty and thus a reduction in the adsorption capacity [29]. Therefore, we believe that 0.6 g/L CDs-TOCNC/SA is the best adsorption condition for Pb2+.

Fig. 4(c) shows the adsorption capacity of Pb2+ by CDs-TOCNC/SA at different adsorption times. As the adsorption time increased from 50 to 200 min, the adsorption rate of Pb2+ increased rapidly and then slowly until the adsorption time reached 300 min and became balanced. Therefore, we believe that the optimal adsorption time is 200 min.

For different initial concentrations of Pb2+, the influence of temperature on the adsorption performance of Pb2+ by CDs-TOCNC/SA is shown in Fig. 4(d). At each initial Pb2+ concentration, the adsorption capacity of CDs-TOCNC/SA for Pb2+ also increased with increasing temperature because the hydration degree of Pb2+ decreased gradually [27]. The maximum adsorption capacity obtained using CDs-TOCNC/SA was 285.71 mg/g.

We studied the competitive adsorption properties of CD-TOCNC/SA for Pb2+ under the condition that the adsorbent dosage remained unchanged (50 mg), but different initial concentrations (50, 100, 150, and 200 mg/L) of coexisting ions were added. As shown in Fig. 4(e), the removal rate of Pb2+ by CDs-TOCNC/SA decreased with increasing solution concentration, while the removal rate of Mn2+ and Cu2+ increased with increasing solution concentration. However, the order of removal rate was Pb2+ > Cu2+ > Mn2+. The reason may be that they have different levels of electronegativity (Pb2+ = 2.33 eV, Cu2+ = 2.0 eV, and Mn2+ = 1.55 eV) [30].

The recyclability of CDs-TOCNC/SA for Pb2+ was analyzed using an adsorption-desorption experiment. As shown in Fig. 4(f), the adsorption efficiency of CDs-TOCNC/SA on Pb2+ decreased slowly with an increase in the number of cycles. It can be seen that the removal efficiency of the initial adsorption is 92.42 %. After five adsorption cycles, the removal rate of Pb2+ decreased to 78.99 %. This indicates that CDs-TOCNC/SA could reuse and adsorb Pb2+. As desorption is usually affected by HCl, the adsorption sites on the surface of the material may be destroyed during desorption, resulting in reduced adsorption capacity [31]. This indicated that the destruction of the chemical properties also affected the properties of the adsorbent itself, but the influence was not significant.

The adsorption amount and time (50–300 min) were measured at pH 5 and 25 °C to explore the mechanism of Pb2+ adsorption by CDs-TOCNC/SA, and the adsorption kinetics were analyzed. Then, the experimental data were fitted with pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, and intra-particle diffusion dynamics models.

The pseudo-first-order is expressed by the following equation:
(4)
Q t
=
Q t
1 −
e
−
k 1
t

The pseudo-second-order is calculated as follows:
(5)
Q t
=
k 2
Q e 2
t
1 +
Q e
k 2
t

The intraparticle diffusion model can be expressed as follows:
(6)
Q t
=
k p
t
1 2
+ C

The pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order, and in-particle diffusion models were used to fit the adsorption kinetic data of CDs-TOCNC/SA. The results are presented in Table 1 and Fig. 5 . The R2 values of the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order processes were 0.8883 and 0.9006, respectively, indicating that the adsorption of Pb2+ by CDs-TOCNC/SA conformed to the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. In addition, the C of the intra-particle diffusion model was not equal to zero, and the R2 values were 0.9888, 0.9861, and 0.9809, respectively. Therefore, CDs-TOCNC/SA was chemically adsorbed on Pb2+, and the interaction of Pb2+ was not a simple diffusion process.
Langmuir and Freundlich models were used to study the relationship between CDs-TOCNC/SA and Pb2+.

The Langmuir model can be represented by the following formula [32]:
(7)
C e
Q e
=
1
K L
Q m
+
C e
Q m
where Ce is the equilibrium Pb2+ concentration (mg/L); Qe is the equilibrium adsorption capacity (mg/g), and KL (L/mg) (L/g) is the Langmuir constant.

The Freundlich model is obtained as the equation:
(8)
ln
Q e
= ln
K F
+
1 n
· ln
C e
where KF [(mg/g) (L/mg)1/n] and n are Freundlich constants.

Fig. 6 shows the CDs-TOCNC/SA adsorption isotherm of Pb2+, and Table 2 lists the parameters. It can be found that at 298 K, the R2 of the Langmuir model is higher than that of the Freundlich model. However, with an increase in temperature, the R2 value of the Freundlich model was higher than that of the Langmuir model. It can be observed that the difference between the Freundlich and Langmuir models is not significant. Therefore, the Pb2+ adsorption process of CDs-TOCNC/SA can be demonstrated by single-layer chemisorption at 298 K, while the Pb2+ adsorption process of CDs-TOCNC/SA can be demonstrated by multilayer adsorption at 308 K and 318 K. This may also explain why the adsorption capacity increased with an increase in temperature. According to Langmuir model parameters, the maximum adsorption capacity was 190.11 mg/g. In addition, because 1/n is between 0 and 1, Pb2+ reacts easily with CDs-TOCNC/SA.

The Pb2+ adsorption properties of CDs-TOCNC/SA and previously reported adsorbents are shown in Table 3 . It can be observed that this material is comparable to other adsorbent materials. Moreover, the adsorption capacity of CDs-TOCNC/SA for Pb2+ is superior to that of other adsorbents; other adsorbents typically have a medium adsorption level. This was probably because the porous structure and carbon points provided more adsorption sites for ion adsorption. In addition, CDs-TOCNC/SA has the advantages of low price; simple green preparation; easy recovery and reuse; and broad application prospects.

Fig. 7 (a) shows the FT-IR spectra of SA, TOCNC, CDs-TOCNC/SA, and CDs-TOCNC/SA + Pb2+. TOCNC have a strong absorption peak due to the OH tensile vibration at 3465 cm−1 [27]. Furthermore, CDs-TOCNC/SA and CDs-TOCNC/SA + Pb2+ have a strong absorption peak at 3350 cm−1 due to OH and NH tensile vibrations [39,40]. In addition, a significant characteristic band (1054 cm−1) associated with the CO band of cellulose was observed for TOCNC, CDs-TOCNC/SA, and CDs-TOCNC/SA + Pb2+. When Pb2+ ions are adsorbed by CDs-TOCNC/SA, the energy-saving peak location of -OH is slightly shifted. However, at approximately 1400 cm−1, the peak intensity of the CO vibration or OH bending vibration of carboxylic acid increased and showed an obvious blue shift, which is probably due to the coordination of Pb2+ with the carboxyl group.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA-50) was used to investigate the thermal stability of CDs-TOCNC/SA. The sample is increased from 45 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min at a N2 flow rate of (35 mL/min) and weight of 15 mg. The change in weight loss (%) was recorded at a temperature of 45–600 °C. The thermal degradation curves of SA, TOCNC, CDs-TOCNC/SA, and CDs-TOCNC/SA + Pb2+ are shown in Fig. 7(c). During the process from 45 °C to 100 °C, there was a slight decrease in the mass of these three substances (no >10 %) due to water loss. The weight loss at 100–200 °C is mainly due to the removal of the interlayer water molecules and internal crystallization of the material [27]. The main stage of cellulose pyrolysis occurred at 200–312 °C. TOCNC exhibited the fastest weightlessness. In this range, TOCNC is decomposed into small molecules of gas and large molecules of condensed volatiles, and the weight loss is significant. The weight loss rate reached its maximum at 304 °C, and the loss was approximately 59 % at 327 °C, which was the highest degradation temperature. TOCNC was finished with a quality percentage of 29 %. The mass of CDs-TOCNC/SA microspheres decreased for the first time at 203.8 °C; this decrease may have been caused by the increase in thermal stability due to the addition of SA. At 304 °C, CDs-TOCNC/SA lost approximately 41 %, which was the fastest degradation temperature, and at 330 °C, it lost approximately 49 %, which was the maximum degradation temperature. Finally, the CDs-TOCNC/SA microspheres were left with a surplus of 34.7 %. It is concluded that CDs-TOCNC/SA has a thermal stability of up to 200 °C, which may be an advantage in the face of hot industrial wastewater.

Fig. 7(d) shows the adsorption-desorption curve of N2 at the temperature of 77 K. The CDs-TOCNC/SA curve in the figure is type IV, indicating a mesoporous structure [42]. The BET surface area of CDs-TOCNC/SA was 5.77 m2/g, and the Langmuir surface area was 8.048 m2/g, which greatly facilitated the adsorption of Pb2+.
CDs-TOCNC/SA emitted blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light (left), and the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly after Pb2+ adsorption (right).

In addition, we studied the fluorescence quenching of CDs-TOCNC/SA at different Pb2+ concentrations (Fig. 7(f)). 1 mg of CDs-TOCNC/SA was added to 5 mL Pb2+ solutions with concentrations of 0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 1, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 mg/L. After standing for 12 h, the adsorption capacity and fluorescence quenching degree of the adsorbents were measured. The adsorption capacity of CDs-TOCNC/SA increased with increasing Pb2+ concentration, but the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually, and there was almost no fluorescence display at 100 mg/L, proving that the adsorption process of Pb2+ by CDs-TOCNC/SA is visual.

To explore the composition of CDs-TOCNC/SA and its adsorption mechanism on Pb2+, we performed XPS spectrometric determination and peak fitting comparison of CDs-TOCNC/SA and CDs-TOCNC/SA + Pb2+. As shown in Fig. 8(a), the elements of CDs-TOCNC/SA were mainly C, N, O, and Ca. After the adsorption of Pb2+, the peak of Ca almost disappeared, while the peak of Pb appeared. This implies that the two may have exchanged ions. In addition, there is a significant difference between the two, since the bond energies of the two Pb 4f peaks in Pb(NO3)2 are 139.2 eV and 144.1 eV, while the binding energies of Pb 4f5/2 and Pb4f7/2 are 143.3 eV and 138.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 8(b)) [43,44]. This indicates that Pb2+ interacts with CDs-TOCNC/SA surface functional groups during the adsorption process. It can be seen that the peaks of Ca 2p1/2 and Ca 2p3/2 are 351.1 eV and 347.4 eV, respectively, and the peaks almost disappear after Pb2+ adsorption by CDs-TOCNC/SA (Fig. 8(c)). Combined with the C1s spectra in Fig. 8(d), it was further demonstrated that ion exchange occurred between Ca2+ and Pb2+ during the adsorption process. It can also be observed that the bond energy of CC (284.5 eV), C-O/C-N (286.1 eV), and C-O/-COOH (288.2 eV) in CDs-TOCNC/SA remained unchanged after adsorption of Pb2+. Fig. 8(e) shows the N1s spectrum, in which the binding energies of NH (399.2 eV ~399.4 eV) and CN (401.2 eV ~ 401.3 eV) have slightly changed. The binding energies of CO (increased from 531.3 eV to 532 eV) and C-OH (increased from 532.7 eV to 533.1 eV) in the O1s spectrum also changed. It can be found that the binding energy of CDs-TOCNC/SA functional groups changes after Pb2+ adsorption [6]. This indicates that any of these functional groups can coordinate with Pb2+.
Based on the above characterization, we can conclude that the CDs-TOCNC/SA adsorption of Pb2+ occurs via a variety of different mechanisms.

(1) Ion exchange: EDS and XPS analyses confirmed the presence of Ca2+ in the adsorbent, and subsequent analyses revealed that the XPS peak of Ca2+ was weakened. This proves that Ca2+ and Pb2+ are the exchanged ions.

(2) Electrostatic adsorption: Pb2+ has electrostatic attraction to the negatively charged groups of CDs-TOCNC/SA. According to the experiment, CDs-TOCNC/SA had a better electrostatic adsorption effect on Pb2+ under weakly acidic conditions.
(3) Intramaterial diffusion: The intramaterial diffusion model demonstrated Pb2+ migration from the main liquid phase to the CDs-TOCNC/SA surface.
(4) Chemical complexation: Fluorescence quenching, XPS, and FT-IR analyses verified the complexation of CDs functional groups on the CDs-TOCNC/SA surface with Pb2+.

In summary, CDs were prepared using a one-pot low-temperature hydrothermal method, and nanocellulose-based fluorescent microspheres were prepared using the electrostatic self-assembly green method. The preparation process of the adsorbent has the advantages of simplicity, good reproducibility, strong adsorption capacity, and wide application; no toxic small-molecule crosslinking agent and artificial polymer are added. CDs-TOCNC/SA microspheres have a porous structure with CDs attached to the surface and emit fluorescence under 365-nm UV light. The theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of the adsorbent was 190.1 mg/g, and the selective and visual adsorption of Pb2+ ions was successfully achieved. The results show that the adsorption of Pb2+ by CDs-TOCNC/SA is single-layer chemical adsorption. The XPS analysis revealed that the main mechanism of Pb2+ adsorption by CDs-TOCNC/SA is cation exchange, electrostatic attraction, ion diffusion, and chemical complexation. Moreover, the CDs-TOCNC/SA circulation was good, and it was convenient to recycle the adsorbent. Therefore, this study successfully developed a green and reusable preparation method for visualizing heavy metal ion adsorbents. However, the adsorption capacity of CDS-TOCNC/SA was not high, and CDs should be stored away from light because it is easy to quench light. In the future, we will further increase the adsorption capacity of adsorbents, increase the fluorescence intensity of CDs, and modify CDs to produce different colors to selectively quench certain ions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
The QUTJBZ Program (No. 2022JBZ01-05) financially supported funding this article.
